steps.title: A door that opens with a code

narration.default: Hotel rooms, office entrances, gym lockers, smart safes — the metal key is gone. You walk up, tap a code on a keypad, and the lock swings open. Today you'll build a tiny version of that. A keypad to type on, a servo to swing the bolt, and a few lines of code to tie them together. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode, and the Membrane Keypad and Servo Motor extensions are added in Sensor Manager. If you haven't done Blink an LED, Control an LED with a Button, and Move a Servo yet, finish those first — this tutorial builds on all three. Move a Servo also walks you through Sensor Manager if extensions are new to you.

Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've done this several times now — setup runs once when the program starts; loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.

Open the Keypad category and drag the setup keypad block into setup. Leave the type on 4 by 4 — four rows of four buttons, sixteen keys in total. Set the row pins to 2, 3, 4, 5 and the column pins to 6, 7, 8, 9. That's eight pins for sixteen keys. Hold on to that thought — we'll see the trick to it in a moment.

steps.title: Wake up the servo on pin 10

narration.default: Same servo pattern as Move a Servo. Make a variable called servo. Drag a 'set servo to' block below the keypad setup and drop a Servo reporter from the Servo category into the value slot — the reporter creates a new servo and your variable now holds a reference to it. Drag a servo attach block right under and set the pin to 10. Now when we say 'go to ninety degrees' the library knows which pin to drive.

steps.title: Sixteen buttons — how does it know?

narration.default: Pause for a moment. Back in the Control an LED with a Button tutorial, one button used one pin. Easy. But this keypad has sixteen buttons and we only gave it eight pins — four rows and four columns. So how does the Arduino know which of the sixteen keys you actually pressed? Hold that question; the next step is the answer.

Here's the trick. The keys sit in a grid — every key is at a place where one row wire crosses one column wire. The Arduino checks the grid thousands of times every second: row by row, column by column, asking 'is anything pressed at this crossing?' If a key is held down, exactly one row and one column light up together — and that pair tells the Arduino which key it was. The keypad library does all that scanning for us. We just ask 'what was pressed?' and we get back a single character.

steps.title: A box for the pressed key

narration.default: Open the Variables category and click Make a Variable. Name it key. This is where we'll store whichever key the user just pressed — could be a digit, could be the star, could be nothing at all if no key is held.

Drag a set variable block into the loop. Pick key on the dropdown. Then drag the read keypad reporter from the Keypad category and drop it into the value slot. Now every time the loop runs, key gets refreshed with whatever is being pressed right now.

Open Control and drag a plain if block — not if-else this time, just if — into the loop, below the set-key line. We'll fill in the diamond slot with the question 'is the pressed key the unlock key?' in the next two steps.

Open Operators and drag an equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if. On the left side, drop the key variable from Variables. On the right side, type the digit one. The keypad gives us a single character each time, so we're asking: 'is the pressed key the digit one?'

Drag a servo write block into the if branch — the body that runs when the question's answer is yes. Set the pin to 10 and the angle to 90. When the user presses the digit one, the servo swings to ninety degrees — the bolt slides open, the door is unlocked.

Take a moment to look at what you've built. Press the digit one and the door unlocks. Press anything else — two, five, the hash, the star, whatever — and nothing happens. There's no else branch, so the program just keeps reading the keypad. Right now the door has only one rule. Next we'll add a second rule so we can lock it again.

Drag a second if block into the loop, below the first one. Two ifs in a row means the program checks both questions every loop, one after the other. The first if handles unlocking; this one will handle locking.
Drag another equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the second if. Drop the key variable on the left, and on the right type the star. The question reads: 'is the pressed key the star?'

Drag another servo write block into the second if branch. Keep the pin on 10 and set the angle to 0. When the user presses the star, the servo swings back to zero — the bolt slides closed, the door is locked again. Two keys, two angles, two states: unlocked and locked.

Drag a delay block to the very bottom of the loop. Set it to 50 milliseconds. Every loop body should end with a small pause — it gives the Arduino breathing room and stops the keypad from registering a single press as dozens of presses in a row.

Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Membrane Keypad. The dialog asks for eight pins — four rows and four columns. Set the row pins to 2, 3, 4, 5 and the column pins to 6, 7, 8, 9 — exactly the same numbers you typed into the setup keypad block. Click Save. A virtual 4 by 4 keypad appears next to the Arduino.

Click the plus button again, choose Servo, set the pin to 10, click Save. The virtual servo appears — a little arm that swings to whatever angle your code asks for. In a real lock it would be glued to a sliding bolt; in the simulator we just watch the arm move.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds to build. The simulator goes live.

Click the digit one on the simulated keypad. The servo arm swings to ninety degrees — unlocked. Click the star. The servo swings back to zero — locked. Press any other key and nothing happens, because no if branch matches. You just built a working keypad-controlled lock.

steps.title: A real lock would chain digits

narration.default: Real keypad locks don't open on a single digit — they want a sequence, like one-two-three-four. To do that, the program has to remember the last few keys in order and compare the whole sequence to the secret code. That needs a list of values held over time — a tool called an array. We'll meet arrays in a later tutorial; for now, one key opens, one key closes, and that's enough to see the idea.

Look at what you just put together. A keypad — sixteen buttons read by only eight pins, thanks to the library scanning rows and columns thousands of times a second. A variable named key that holds whichever character was pressed. The equals block comparing characters to decide what to do. Two ifs in a row, each with its own action. A servo whose angle stands in for two states: zero degrees means locked, ninety means unlocked. Programs that read input, decide, and move something in the world are everywhere — keyless car entry, ATM keypads, smart safes, hotel room doors. You can write them now.
